source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, ed. William Crooke (1920 edition of Tod's 1829 original), Book IV Chapter 11
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23). Source: ibiblio.org 'British Raj' collection (https://www.ibiblio.org/britishraj/Tod1/bk04ch11.html), a plain-text transcription of William Crooke's 1920 annotated edition of James Tod's Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan (first published 1829-32). Tod died in 1835; the work is unambiguously public domain in all jurisdictions, and the ibiblio.org 'British Raj' project states the collection is presumed public domain under both US and UK copyright rules. Full chapter text (Chapter 11, 'Rana Partap Singh, A.D. 1572-97') was fetched and verified directly.

content.text: Partap succeeded to the titles and renown of an illustrious house, but without a capital, without resources, his kindred and clans dispirited by reverses. Yet possessed of the noble spirit of his race, he meditated the recovery of Chitor and the restoration of Mewar's power, even as the Mogul arrayed against him princes of his own faith and blood -- Marwar, Amber, Bikaner, and even his own brother Sagarji, who deserted him for the price of Chitor's title.
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: To commemorate the desolation of Chitor, Partap interdicted to himself and his successors every article of luxury or pomp until Chitor's honour should be redeemed: gold and silver dishes were laid aside for leaf-plates, beds of straw replaced beds of comfort, and beards were left untouched. He commanded that the martial drums, which always led the van in battle or procession, should follow in the rear instead -- a sign of Mewar's fallen fortune that survived among his descendants long after.
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: When Raja Man Singh of Amber, whose family had married into the Mughal court, visited Partap at Kumbhalmer, Partap refused to dine with him, sending word: "he could not eat with a Rajput who gave his sister to a Turk." Raja Man Singh withdrew in fury, vowing "If I do not humble your pride, my name is not Man" -- an insult to Akbar's court that helped precipitate the war culminating at Haldighat.
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: At the Battle of Haldighat (18 June 1576), Partap led the crimson banner into the hottest part of the field, his steed Chetak rearing with one foot upon the Mogul elephant as the prince's lance was leveled at Prince Salim. Marked by the royal umbrella he would not lay aside, Partap was thrice rescued and nearly overwhelmed when the Jhala chief Mana seized the insignia of Mewar and drew the enemy upon himself, giving his life so that Partap could be forced from the field. Of twenty-two thousand Rajputs who fought that day, only eight thousand left the field alive.
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: Fleeing alone on the wounded Chetak, Partap was closely pursued by two Mogul horsemen until a lone rider called out in his native tongue, "Ho! rider of the blue horse" -- his estranged brother Sakta, who had fought for Akbar but now turned to slay the pursuers. Chetak died of his wounds at that spot; the brothers embraced for the first time in their lives, and Sakta was pardoned by Prince Salim on the promise that he had only defended his brother, "The burthen of a kingdom is on my brother's shoulders, nor could I witness his danger without defending him from it."
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: During the years of guerrilla resistance that followed, Partap's family was repeatedly in danger of capture; on one occasion the faithful Bhils of Kava carried them in wicker baskets and hid them in the tin mines of Jawara. In one privation, his daughter's cry from hunger -- as a wild cat snatched the last reserved portion of grass-flour cake from the family -- was, in the chronicler's words, the one thing that "unmanned" Partap after he had watched his sons fall in battle without emotion, "for this the Rajput was born."
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: Partap's minister Bhama Shah placed at the prince's disposal his family's accumulated wealth -- said to be enough to maintain twenty-five thousand men for twelve years -- enabling Partap to surprise the Mogul garrisons and recover Kumbhalmer, Udaipur, and nearly all of Mewar except Chitor itself. On his deathbed, Partap made his chiefs swear "by the throne of Bappa Rawal" that they would not build sumptuous dwellings until Mewar had recovered her full independence. Tod's annals close the chapter: "Haldighat is the Thermopylae of Mewar; the field of Dawer her Marathon."
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11

content.text: This entry compiles verified passages from James Tod's Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan (1829), Book IV, Chapter 11, 'Rana Partap Singh, A.D. 1572-97', as transcribed in William Crooke's 1920 annotated edition and hosted by the ibiblio.org 'British Raj' public-domain text collection. Tod's work is unambiguously public domain (author died 1835); the ibiblio.org transcription is presented for non-commercial educational and research use.
content.source_name: James Tod, Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan, Book IV Ch. 11
